title: The UK, Its Institutions and Your Involvement
overview: The United Kingdom is a democracy built on the rule of law and shared public institutions. Citizens and government work together to protect rights, maintain order, and improve life for everyone. Understanding how institutions operate—Parliament, courts, police, councils, and media—helps people take part confidently in society.

study_sections.heading: Government and Parliament
study_sections.content: Parliament makes laws and holds government to account. Government runs the country. The Monarch is Head of State; the Prime Minister leads government. Parliament consists of Commons (elected MPs) and Lords (appointed members).

study_sections.heading: Devolved and Local Government
study_sections.content: Devolved administrations govern Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Local councils manage education, housing, transport, and waste collection closer to communities.

study_sections.heading: Law and Justice
study_sections.content: The rule of law ensures everyone is equal before the law. Police protect the public. Courts (Magistrates', Crown, Supreme) interpret law through independent judges and juries.

study_sections.heading: Rights and Protections
study_sections.content: Human Rights Act 1998 and Equality Act 2010 protect freedom and fairness. Citizens must obey laws, pay taxes, and serve on juries when called.

study_sections.heading: Elections and Participation
study_sections.content: General elections held every five years using secret ballot. Citizens aged 18+ can vote. People can join campaigns, contact MPs, or volunteer to influence decisions.

study_sections.heading: Media and Democracy
study_sections.content: Free media inform citizens and hold leaders accountable. Active citizenship includes voting, volunteering, and supporting communities responsibly.

key_facts: Prime Minister is Head of Government
Citizens must obey law and pay taxes
memory_hook: UK institutions as a tree: Parliament (trunk making laws), Government (branches executing), Courts (roots ensuring fairness), Police (bark protecting), Media (leaves spreading info), Citizens (soil nourishing through taxes/votes). Each part vital—remove one and tree weakens. You're the soil—without citizen participation, nothing grows!

questions.prompt: Who is Head of State in UK?
What are two Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: House of Commons and House of Lords.

questions.prompt: What is highest court in UK?
How often are general elections held?
Who can vote in UK elections?

options: Taking part in public life responsibly

supportive_messages.high_score: Excellent! You understand UK institutions brilliantly! 🏛️
supportive_messages.medium_score: Good work! Review institutions and participation! 📚
